---
title: "Bouncing Back Better?"
subtitle: "Evaluating the economic impact of the 2004 Aceh tsunami"
deck-author: "Carlos Mendez"
deck-author-url: "https://carlos-mendez.org"
institute: "Nagoya University (GSID)"
date: today
date-format: long
key-results:
  - { num: "−7.9%", cap: "output shock in 2005" }
  - { num: "+6.3pp/yr", cap: "recovery premium 2006–08" }
  - { num: "+18.3%", cap: "synthetic-control gap by 2012" }
format:
  revealjs:
    output-file: index.html
    # NOT embed-resources: RevealChalkboard is incompatible with self-contained
    # output, so the deck ships index.html + slides_files/ (Hugo serves this fine —
    # the tutorial.html precedent). Figures are referenced ../python_did_sc_tsunami_*.png.
    theme: [default, site-brand.scss]
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    center: true
    slide-number: c/t
    menu: true
    chalkboard: true
    preview-links: auto
    overview: true
    progress: true
    history: true
    transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    fig-align: center
    auto-stretch: true
    highlight-style: github
    link-external-icon: false
---

# The Tension {.divider background-color="#d97757"}

[Act I]{.act}

## A magnitude-9.1 quake, a wave 9 km inland, and ~130,000 lives lost in one province

A third of Aceh's coastline, flooded in one morning. Then the **largest disaster-reconstruction effort ever** — about **USD 7.0 billion**, well spent.

. . .

A decade later: was Aceh **richer or poorer** than without the wave?

::: {.notes}
The hook is the genuine ambiguity. Catastrophe destroys; well-spent aid rebuilds — which force won? The aid came through an unusually low-corruption agency, worth roughly 150% of Aceh's damages, spent unusually well. The point of the talk is not just "what happened" but "how could you ever measure it credibly".
:::

## You only ever observe the world where the tsunami *did* happen

To answer it, we need Aceh's **counterfactual** — the output it would have had with no tsunami. That world is never observed; it must be *estimated*.

. . .

Our target is the **ATT** — the average effect of the treatment on the treated:

$$\text{ATT} = E[\,Y(1) - Y(0) \mid D = 1\,]$$

[The effect *on the flooded districts* — not on some randomly chosen district.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
This is the fundamental problem of causal inference. Y(1) is observed for flooded Aceh; Y(0) is not. DiD estimates Y(0) from the control group's trend; synthetic control estimates it from a weighted blend of donor districts. Both target the ATT.
:::

## The wave's path was geography, not choice — that is what makes it a natural experiment

**Elevation, vegetation, and offshore depth** decided which coast flooded — read off satellite maps, not chosen by economics. So flooded vs spared is plausibly *unrelated* to a district's economic prospects.

. . .

[A note on the data.]{.objection} Everything here runs on **synthetic, calibrated** data — tuned to reproduce Heger & Neumayer (2019)'s signs, significance, and approximate magnitudes. Learn the *methods*, not new facts about Aceh.

::: {.notes}
Treatment is defined off DLR/ZKI and Dartmouth Flood Observatory maps made 1–5 days after the wave — exogenous geographic happenstance. The synthetic-data caveat is stated up front for honesty; Section 11 of the post audits it column by column.
:::

# The Investigation {.divider background-color="#6a9bcc"}

[Act II]{.act}

## One disaster, measured at two grains — district GDP and sub-district night-lights

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### District GDP
- 125 Sumatran districts
- annual, 1999–2012 (1,750 rows)
- **10 flooded Aceh** districts treated
- outcome: real GDP growth (oil & gas excluded)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Sub-district night-lights
- 276 Aceh sub-districts
- satellite luminosity (DMSP-OLS, 0–63)
- continuous *dose*: share flooded
- outcome: log-luminosity growth
:::
::::

[Only **10 treated units** — the recurring source of statistical caution.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
National GDP would barely flinch at a one-province shock, so we need sub-national data at two grains. District GDP gives the headline DiD; the finer night-lights panel lets us ask whether the harder-hit places rebounded more.
:::

## Parallel before 2005, then a dive and an overshoot

![Treated (orange) vs control (blue) group-mean growth: lockstep before the tsunami, then the treated line plunges to ≈ −0.027 and overshoots to ≈ +0.124 in 2007.](../python_did_sc_tsunami_group_means.png)

::: {.notes}
This is the picture DiD was invented for. Pre-2005 the two lines move together — the visual signature of parallel trends. At the tsunami the treated line dives; then it over-shoots before settling back toward the control. The eye is convinced; the rest of the talk quantifies it and attaches a margin of error.
:::

## A single "after" hides the story

The simplest DiD splits time into before/after and takes the difference of the two changes:

$$\widehat{\text{DiD}} = \big(\bar{g}_{\text{treat, after}} - \bar{g}_{\text{treat, before}}\big) - \big(\bar{g}_{\text{ctrl, after}} - \bar{g}_{\text{ctrl, before}}\big)$$

. . .

Pooled DiD: **+0.0125**, insignificant (p = 0.38).

One "after" blends the **2005 crash** with the **2006–08 boom** — they cancel.

::: {.notes}
The pooled estimate is not wrong, it is uninformative. The fix is to let the effect vary over time — split the post period into event-time windows. That is the paper's headline specification.
:::

## −7.9% in 2005, then +6.3 pp/yr faster in 2006–08

``` {.python code-line-numbers="1-2|3"}
m = pf.feols("gdp_growth ~ D_pre + D_2005 + D_recov + D_post | district_id + year",
             data=df, vcov={"CRV1": "district_id"})
m.coef().round(4)   # D_2005 = −0.0792 · D_recov = +0.0628
```

| Event-time window | Estimate | Conley-HAC SE | Sig. |
|---|---:|---:|:--:|
| Pre-tsunami (2003–04) | +0.0172 | 0.0159 | ns |
| Tsunami (2005) | [−0.0792]{.key} | 0.0240 | \*\*\* |
| Recovery (2006–08) | [+0.0628]{.key} | 0.0244 | \*\* |
| Post-recovery (2009–12) | +0.0114 | 0.0146 | ns |

[Flat pre-trend → parallel trends holds. The gain persists but doesn't compound → a *permanently higher* path.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
Three takeaways. The pre-tsunami coefficient is small and insignificant — parallel trends survives its formal placebo. The 2005 coefficient is −0.0792 (≈ 8% slower the year the wave hit). The recovery coefficient is +0.0628; three years of that premium more than erase the one-year loss. This is the paper's signature "recovery beyond the counterfactual trend".
:::

## The event study shows *why* the pooled average misled

![Treated-minus-control effect by period (95% CIs): baseline and pre-trend sit on zero, 2005 collapses to −0.079, recovery rebounds to +0.063, then drifts back but stays positive.](../python_did_sc_tsunami_event_study.png)

::: {.notes}
The single "after" dummy averaged the deep-negative 2005 point with the high recovery point and got a muted, insignificant number. The event study shows the whole arc — a flat pre-trend (identification visibly satisfied), a sharp collapse, a significant rebound, and a persistent higher level.
:::

## Not a denominator artifact — per-capita recovery is even larger

A worry: maybe "growth" rose only because population fell (130,000 deaths and displacement). Re-running the DiD on **GDP per capita**:

. . .

[+0.0827]{.bignum}

[recovery coefficient on per-capita growth, p < 0.01 — output per person rose, not just totals]{.bignum-label}

::: {.notes}
In per-capita terms there is no significant 2005 loss (output and population fell together that year), but the recovery gain strengthens to +0.0827. Fewer people then shared a rebuilt, better-capitalized economy. The effect survives dividing by population.
:::

## The harder-hit rebounded more — and only the worst-hit fifth significantly

![Night-lights dose-response: continuous period effects (left) and effect by flood-intensity quintile (right) — only Q5, the most heavily flooded sub-districts, rebounds significantly.](../python_did_sc_tsunami_nightlights_dose.png)

| Quintile | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 (worst-hit) |
|---|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Recovery effect | +0.0010 | +0.0010 | +0.0009 | +0.0008 | [+0.0018]{.key}\*\* |

::: {.notes}
Each unit of population-share flooded buys +0.016/year extra luminosity growth in recovery (p < 0.001). But the average is not spread evenly — only the top intensity quintile shows a significant rebound. The effect concentrates exactly where the damage, and therefore the reconstruction spending, was greatest.
:::

## A synthetic Aceh, built from 76 donors, tracks the pre-2005 path almost exactly

Synthetic control picks non-negative weights $w$ that sum to one to minimize pre-treatment mismatch:

$$w^{\ast} = \arg\min_{w}\ (X_1 - X_0 w)^{\top} V (X_1 - X_0 w) \quad \text{s.t.}\quad w_j \ge 0,\ \textstyle\sum_j w_j = 1$$

![Synthetic Aceh tracks the real path before 2005 (pre-RMSE 0.485); afterward the actual line pulls above.](../python_did_sc_tsunami_synthetic_control.png)

::: {.notes}
A pre-RMSE of 0.485 against GDP levels near 200 means synthetic Aceh shadows the real thing almost perfectly before 2005 — which is what licenses trusting it as a counterfactual afterward. The top weight is only 0.13; the counterfactual rests on many modest donors, not one look-alike.
:::

## +18.3% above its no-tsunami twin by 2012 — and the gap opens only after the wave

![The treated-minus-synthetic gap: indistinguishable from zero before 2005, then steadily positive.](../python_did_sc_tsunami_sc_gap.png)

[Two very different methods — DiD and synthetic control — now agree: flooded Aceh ended materially above where it was heading.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
By 2012 actual Aceh is at 370.9 vs the synthetic 295.0 — a +18.3% gap. The gap plot isolates the divergence: zero before treatment, opening afterward. Triangulation across independent methods is a far stronger claim than any one method alone.
:::

## All 10 treated units sit in one corner of the map

![Longitude–latitude scatter of every Sumatran district: the 10 flooded (treated) units, in orange, cluster on Aceh's far north-west coast.](../python_did_sc_tsunami_spatial_map.png)

[Near things are more related than distant things — their growth shocks are *not* independent draws.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
When treated units are packed together, a good monsoon, a regional price swing, or the reconstruction boom itself hits them together. The default standard error assumes independence, so it counts more truly-independent information than the data contain — and reports SEs that are too small.
:::

## The point estimate never moved — only our honesty about it did

Moran's I on the residuals is **+0.065** (permutation p = 0.003): nearby districts' growth is significantly correlated. The fix is a **Conley spatial-HAC** standard error.

| Recovery effect | Estimate | Naive SE | Conley-HAC SE | t(HAC) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 2006–08 | +0.0628 | 0.0146 | [0.0244]{.key} | +2.57 |

[Same +0.0628 in every column. The SE inflates 1.68× — downgrading a spurious \*\*\* to an honest \*\*.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
Under the naive error the recovery effect would have a t above 4 and look significant at 1%. Under the honest Conley-HAC error its t is 2.57 — significant at 5%, not 1%. A careless analyst would have overstated the confidence threefold. The SE is stable across the 25–100 km cutoff range the paper uses.
:::

# The Resolution {.divider background-color="#00d4c8"}

[Act III]{.act}

## Four methods, one story: recovery beyond the counterfactual trend

::: {.incremental}
- **DiD** — −7.9% in 2005, +6.3 pp/yr in 2006–08, on a permanently higher path
- **Event study** — flat pre-trend, sharp collapse, significant rebound
- **Night-lights** — the dose-response: only the worst-hit quintile rebounds
- **Synthetic control** — +18.3% above the no-tsunami Aceh by 2012
:::

[Triangulation, not a single regression, is what makes the claim credible.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
Four tools built on different assumptions all point the same way. That convergence — not the precision of any one estimate — is the strongest part of the evidence.
:::

## Well-governed mega-reconstruction can bend a poor region's path upward

The lesson is **not** "disasters are good" — 130,000 people died. It is that a localized catastrophe followed by **large, well-spent aid** can leave a poor region permanently better off.

. . .

Aid ≈ **150% of damages** · low-corruption agency · "built back better."

*That combination — not the wave — bent the path upward.*

::: {.notes}
For disaster policy the design lesson matters as much as the result: a credible evaluation needs exogenous exposure (geography, not choice), a finer-than-national unit of analysis, and spatially honest standard errors.
:::

## The strongest objection — and the answer

[Objection.]{.objection} The data are **synthetic**, and there are only **10 treated districts** — point estimates are fragile, standard errors wide.

. . .

[Response.]{.rebuttal}

- Synthetic data are *calibrated* to the paper — audited column by column.
- Small-N met head-on: **flat pre-trend**, **null placebo**, **Conley-HAC** errors.
- Caveats narrow the claim — they don't overturn it.

::: {.notes}
Steelman first: this is a single, exceptionally well-funded case study on simulated data with a tiny treatment group. The honest reply is that every credibility check — placebo, pre-trend, spatial inference — has been run and survived, and the conclusion is stated as evidence about well-governed mega-reconstruction, not disaster aid in general.
:::

## Five numbers to remember

| Number | Value |
|---|---:|
| 2005 output shock | [−0.0792]{.key}\*\*\* |
| 2006–08 recovery premium (per year) | +0.0628\*\* |
| Synthetic-control gap by 2012 | +18.3% |
| Moran's I (spatial autocorrelation) | +0.065 |
| Recovery SE: naive → Conley-HAC | 0.0146 → 0.0244 |

[And five lessons: let evolving effects evolve · triangulate · satellite data unlock localized questions · clustered treatment needs honest inference · mind the small print.]{.comment}

::: {.notes}
Close on the numbers, then the five lessons. One: the pooled 2×2 was an insignificant +0.0125 — only event-time windows revealed the −0.079 collapse and +0.063 overshoot, so let evolving effects evolve. Two: DiD, event study, dose-response, and synthetic control all pointed the same way; agreement beats any one estimate. Three: night-lights exposed a dose-response invisible at the district grain. Four: Conley spatial errors downgraded the recovery from a spurious *** to an honest ** without touching the point estimate. Five: ten treated districts on synthetic data, identified by an assumption — strong evidence, stated with its caveats. Next steps in the post: staggered-adoption DiD estimators, prediction intervals for the synthetic control, a wider donor pool.
:::

# A poor region, well-governed reconstruction, a permanently higher path. {.divider background-color="#141413"}
